coredata.dc:title: Preparation of “green” carbon quantum dots coated core-shell type pearl pigment Kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO 2-x
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Yue
Yang, Yuxiang
Liu, Wenwen
Kang, Shizhao
Huang, Yan
Zhao, Yi
Dai, Anbang
Carlini, Riccardo
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, the green and environmentally friendly pearlescent pigment particles of the core-shell type kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x composite were prepared via sol-gel deposition method, the surface of kieselguhr coated by TiO2, and then the surface of the composites was coated with TiO2-x by the technique of co-precipitation and the route of pyrolysis of the hydrazine salt. Orthogonal design method was employed to study the effects of different operating parameters on properties of the core-shell type kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x composite. Due to characteristics of large specific surface with a lot of hydroxyl groups, the composite pigment were coated with Carbon dots with the assistance of (3-aminopropyl)trimethoxysilane (APS). Thus, the prepared composite pigment was mixed with citric acid and APS followed by hydrothermal reaction at 180 °C to generate carbon quantum dots coated composite pigment (kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x@CDs). The obtained kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x@CDs composite were characterized by X ray powder diffraction (XRD), element analysis, Raman spectra analysis, Hitachi s4800 field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), X ray photoelectron spectroscopy (EDS) and TG-DTA respectively. And the color instrumental measurement was used to determine L*, a* and b* value of a pigment to evaluate the color brightness of the core-shell type kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x composite. In addition, the MTT method, also known as MTT colorimetry, is a way of detecting cell survival and growth, which was employed to study in vitro cytotoxicity of their composite particles against mouse peritoneal macrophages for evaluation of their green application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pearlescent pigment
Co-precipitation
Coated composite pigment
Nanoparticles in green application
Kieselguhr@TiO2

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

Experimental

The main experimental instrument
Experimental section
Kieselguhr surface activation treatment
Preparation of kieselguhr@TiO2
Preparation of kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x
Preparation of kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x@CDs
Sample characterization
Results and discussion
Carbon, hydrogen and nitrogen (C, H and N) chemical elemental analysis
XRD characterization of the precursor
Effects of precursor molecules on HRSEM image and color parameters of the obtained samples
Thermogravimetry-differential thermal analysis of precursor
Raman spectrum analysis of the calcined predecessor
Powder X-ray diffraction analysis of calcined predecessor
Characterization of the core-shell type kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x composite particles prepared by the typical preparation process
Powder X-ray diffraction analysis of kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x composite particles
X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis of kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x composite particles
Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) measurements of Kieselguhr@TiO2 and Kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x
High-resolution scanning electron microscopy characterization of Kieselguhr, Kieselguhr@TiO2 and Kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x
Effects of the conditions parameters on the morphology and color intensity
Effects of the acid/TiCl3 ratio on the morphology and color intensity
Effects of pH on the morphology and color intensity
Effects of the amount of hydrazine hydrate on the morphology and color intensity
Effects of the concentration of sodium hydroxide on the morphology and color intensity
Effects of the reaction temperature on the morphology and color intensity
Effects of the calcination temperature on the morphology and color intensity
Carbon dots coated composite pigment (kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x@CDs)
In vitro cytotoxicity against mouse peritoneal macrophages of kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x@CDs composite particles
Conclusions
Acknowledgements
References

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of Preparation Process of kieselguhr@TiO2
Scheme of Preparation Process of kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x
XRD pattern of the precursor.
HRSEM images of the product obtained by controlling the ratio of succinic acid to TiCl3 at 2:1 but different reaction time (a) 0.5 h, (b) 1.0 h, (c) 1.5 h, (d) 2.0 h
Thermogravimetry-differential thermal analysis (TG-DTA) of precursor with succinic acid as the acid source (a) DTA (b) TG.
Raman spectrum of calcined predecessor.
X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the calcined predecessor.
X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of Kieselguhr@TiO2 and Kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x
X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectral peaks of Ti2p and Si2p on the surface of kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x
Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) patterns of kieselguhr@TiO2 and kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x
High-resolution scanning electron microscopy (HRSEM) images of kieselguhr (a and b), kieselguhr@TiO2 (c and d) and kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x (e and f).
X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of titanium oxides synthetized at different acid/TiCl3 ratio: (a) 1.6:1 (b) 1.8:1 (c) 2:1 (d) 2.2:1 (e) 2.4:1.
High-resolution scanning electron microscopy (HRSEM) images of titanium oxides synthetized at different acid/TiCl3 ratio: (a) 1.6:1 (b) 1.8:1 (c) 2:1 (d) 2.2:1 (e) 2.4:1.
High-resolution scanning electron microscopy (HRSEM) images of samples obtained at different pH:(a) pH = 5.5 (b) pH = 5.0 (c) pH = 4.5 (d) pH = 4.0 (e) pH = 3.5.
X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of titanium oxides synthetized with different amounts of hydrazine hydrate: (a) 6 mL (b) 8 mL (c) 10 mL (d) 12 mL (e) 15 mL.
High-resolution scanning electron microscopy (HRSEM) patterns of titanium oxides synthetized with different amounts of hydrazine hydrate (85.0% w/w): (a) 6 mL (b) 8 mL (c) 10 mL (d) 12 mL (e) 15 mL.
High-resolution scanning electron microscopy (HRSEM) patterns of the samples prepared at different reaction temperature: (a) 25 °C, (b) 35 °C, (c) 45 °C, (d) 55 °C, (e) 65 °C.
High-resolution scanning electron microscopy (HRSEM) images of the samples calcined at different temperatures:(a) 600 °C, (b) 650 °C, (c) 700 °C, (d) 750 °C.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths, (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of bare CDs and kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x@CDs at 360 nm excitation wavelength, (c) Images of fluorescent CDs and kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x@CDs solid powder samples irradiated by a 365 nm UV lamp.
Cell viabilities of mouse peritoneal macrophages after incubation with different concentrations of kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x@CDs for 24 and 48 h.
Elemental analysis of resulting precursor molecules at different reaction times.
Indexation results of X-ray powder diffraction pattern of the precursor.
Color parameters of samples synthetized by different reaction time.
Experimental data and calculated results of X-ray diffraction pattern of TiO2 in calcined predecessor.
Experimental data and calculated results of X-ray diffraction pattern of Ti2O3 in calcined predecessor.
Fitting peaks and their peak areas of the Ti2p peaks of titanium oxide.
Color parameters of samples prepared by different acid/TiCl3 ratio.
Color parameters of samples prepared by different pH.
Color parameters of samples prepared by different amounts of hydrazine hydrate.
Color parameters of samples synthetized by different temperature.
Color parameters of samples prepared by different calcination temperature.

head.ce:title: Preparation of “green” carbon quantum dots coated core-shell type pearl pigment Kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO 2-x

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science & Technology, Shanghai, 200237, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
East China University of Science & Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science & Technology, Shanghai, 200237, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Shanghai Institute of Technology, 100 Haiquan Road, Shanghai, 201418, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Shanghai Institute of Technology, 100 Haiquan Road, Shanghai, 201418, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry and Industrial Chemistry Department, Genoa University, 16146, Genova, Italy
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry and Industrial Chemistry Department, Genoa University, 16146, Genova, Italy

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science & Technology, Shanghai, 200237, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
East China University of Science & Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, the green and environmentally friendly pearlescent pigment particles of the core-shell type kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x composite were prepared via sol-gel deposition method, the surface of kieselguhr coated by TiO2, and then the surface of the composites was coated with TiO2-x by the technique of co-precipitation and the route of pyrolysis of the hydrazine salt. Orthogonal design method was employed to study the effects of different operating parameters on properties of the core-shell type kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x composite. Due to characteristics of large specific surface with a lot of hydroxyl groups, the composite pigment were coated with Carbon dots with the assistance of (3-aminopropyl)trimethoxysilane (APS). Thus, the prepared composite pigment was mixed with citric acid and APS followed by hydrothermal reaction at 180 °C to generate carbon quantum dots coated composite pigment (kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x@CDs).

The obtained kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x@CDs composite were characterized by X ray powder diffraction (XRD), element analysis, Raman spectra analysis, Hitachi s4800 field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), X ray photoelectron spectroscopy (EDS) and TG-DTA respectively. And the color instrumental measurement was used to determine L*, a* and b* value of a pigment to evaluate the color brightness of the core-shell type kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x composite. In addition, the MTT method, also known as MTT colorimetry, is a way of detecting cell survival and growth, which was employed to study in vitro cytotoxicity of their composite particles against mouse peritoneal macrophages for evaluation of their green application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon black and iron black have been used in the coloring of cosmetics since the early 1900s and are also currently used as coloring agents also in any skin product. However, carbon black inevitably contains 3, 4-benzopyrene non-uniformly dispersed. Meanwhile, iron black has the disadvantages of low heat resistance, magnetism, the tendency to aggregate and a poor thermal stability. To solve these problems, researchers developed a new black pigment, namely black titanium oxide (TiO2-x), also known as titanium black, which is a low-cost titanium oxide and mixed-valence titanium oxides. In addition, black titanium oxide has other significant advantages that contribute to its wide use as a black pigment: its non-toxicity, pure black color, good heat resistance, water infiltration and being environmentally friendly.

Black titanium oxide (TiO2-x, x = 0.2–0.35), also known as titanium black, is a mixed valence titanium oxide, such as TiO, Ti2O3, TinO2n-1 (1 ≤ n ≤ 10), etc. [1], and there are at least seven different stable oxides, which follow the general formula TinO2n-1. Compared with TiO2, titanium black is a kind of low-valence suboxide state with the valence of titanium elements equal to +2, +3, etc. In addition, it has a certain degree of reduction, and more surface oxygen vacancies. The surface energy of nano-titanium black is larger than that of nano-TiO2, and its surface activity and polarity are also stronger.

The micron and nano-scale size black titanium oxide is a “green” black pigment with strong coloring power and great perspectives [2,3], as titanium black meets the food-grade safety requirements, it can be used for an entrance passage for coloring products, environmental protection and non-toxic products, as well as in the food industry, cosmetics and beauty products industry [4], such as black mascara, black eyebrow pencil, black eyeliner, etc.

TiO2 is the most stable white powder with good insulation properties and a variety of crystal structures, which is often used as pearlescent coating due to its distinctive optical properties. TiO2-x is a typical mixed valence titanium oxide [5–7], which has a face-centered cubic lattice. This kind of golden yellow substance with metallic luster is a non-stoichiometric compound, i.e., the lattice allows the presence of O and Ti vacancies, and the composition range is TiO0.828±0.002-TiO1.185±0.004, with the lattice parameter a = 0.417 nm ± 0.0005 nm. Also, with the increase of the oxygen index, the lattice constant decreases.

As a carrier material, the kieselguhr is the biological quartzite coming from of fossilized diatoms: the silicon properties detectable in microorganisms remain in the final product.. Its main chemical components are amorphous SiO2 with a minor amount of Al2O3, Fe2O3, CaO and organic matter, etc. [8,9] In addition, its pH is neutral and its fineness is 120–1200 mesh. Due to its properties of adsorption, strong absorption capacity, light weight, chemical stability, physical light and soft, high porosity, wear-resistance, acid-resistance, heat [10] and electrical insulation, large specific surface area, high oil absorption, etc., the kieselguhr is a good pigment carrier and filler. When the kieselguhr particles are coated with nano-TiO2 and the optical thickness of the TiO2 coating is in the range of 80–150 nm, constructive interference occurs across most of the incident spectrum, reflecting approximately 30–40% of the light in the bluish white to yellowish white region of the incident spectrum, which is characteristic of pearlescent pigments [11].

The core-shell type kieselguhr@TiO2 composite paint can improve the brushability and enhance the adhesion of the coating. Furthermore, it is chemically inert, non-toxic and inexpensive, thus the kieselguhr can play a very good role in coating applications and the paint technology field.

Today, carbon quantum dots (CDs) are a new type of carbon nanomaterials with a size below 10 nm. It is an unknown fluorescent carbon nanomaterial first discovered by Xu et al. [1].in 2004. Compared with traditional semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes, luminescent CDs have high water solubility, strong chemical stability, easy functionalization, resistance to photobleaching, excellent biological properties, and good biocompatibility. But, how to coat CDs upon core-shell type kieselguhr@TiO2 composite particles to increase their pearlescent luster has not ever reported so far.

In this study, the kieselguhr was first coated with nano-scale TiO2 through sol-gel deposition. Then, a co-precipitation with a hydrazine salt pyrolysis route were used to perform coating of nano-scale non-stoichiometric TiO2-x on the kieselguhr@TiO2 in order to produce the pearlescent pigment particles of the core-shell type kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x composite. The effects of different acid/TiCl3 ratio, pH value, the concentration of sodium hydroxide, different hydrazine hydrate amounts, reaction temperature, reaction time and calcination temperature on the final coating morphology were investigated through an orthogonal design method.

Subsequently, a facile route by hydrothermal reaction of kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x composite, (3-aminopropyl)trimethoxysilane(APS) and a single precursor, citric acid, at 180 °C produced CDs coated kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x. This method is extremely simple and economical, and provides a feasible route for mass production of highly emissive CDs coated core-shell type pearl pigment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental
Raw materials and chemical reagents
ce:section.ce:para: Kieselguhr, Shengzhou Huali Kieselguhr Products Co. Ltd, Shengzhou county, Zhenjiang province, China; Tetra-tert-butyl orthotitanate (C16H36O4Ti), titanium trichloride (AR), hydrazine hydrate (85.0% w/w), hyddrochloride; Glacial acetic acid; sodium hydroxide; succinic acid; anhydrous ethanol (AR grade above all, 99%, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.)

ce:section.ce:section-title: The main experimental instrument
ce:section.ce:para: DZF-6050 vacuum oven, laboratory equipment Co., Ltd. Shanghai Jing Hong; BT50-1 J peristaltic pump, Baoding Lange constant pump; BT4A1601 type constant flow pump, DICAWOP24000C210E type pH controller, KQ2200E ultrasonic cleaner, Kunshan Ultrasonic Instrument Co., Ltd.; SK2-2-12 tube resistance furnace, Shanghai Yafeng Furnace Industry Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental section
Kieselguhr surface activation treatment
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, kieselguhr contain soluble impurities, so it have to be pre-acidified before use. 100 g of kieselguhr was added into a 500 mL three-necked flask, 200 mL of deionized water and 100 mL of concentrated hydrochloric acid was added successively. This solution was refluxed under magnetic stirring for 5 h to remove the acid-soluble matter therein. Then filtered under vacuum, washed with deionized water until no Cl− residue left measured by no precipitates were found as the solution of AgNO3 was added., dried at 100 °C, afterwards, the purified kieselguhr was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of kieselguhr@TiO2
ce:section.ce:para: 6.6 mL tetra-tert-butyl orthotitanate was added into 50 mL of absolute ethanol (containing 0.5 mL 37% hydrochloric acid) under magnetic stirring; 1.6 g Zhejiang kieselguhr was mixed into 150 mL of deionized water with ultrasonic device. The mixture was transferred into a three-necked flask of 250 mL under magnetic stirring. The ethanol solution of tetra-tert-butyl orthotitanate was then added into three-necked flask through the constant flow pump, the pH of solution was controlled using 0.5 mol/L HAc solution for an hour to maintain its value around 2.3; then the obtained product was filtered and dried at 90 °C. Afterwards, the dried product was ground into powders and calcined in a muffle furnace at a heating rate of 7 °C/min for 3 h at 500 °C to give the obtained kieselguhr@TiO2. The scheme of preparation of kieselguhr@TiO2 was described as follows (see Fig. 1 ):

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical preparation process, 100 mL deionized water and 1.0 g of kieselguhr@TiO2 was mixed in a beaker under ultrasonic condition, followed the mixture and succinic acid solution (0.59 g in 50 mL deionized water) was transferred to the three neck flask under magnetic stirring in an atmosphere of nitrogen. 4.49 g TiCl3 solution was put into the reaction system with a peristaltic pump, then a certain amount of 1 mol/L NaOH was dropped into the reaction system under controlled pH conditions at pH of 5.5 by using pH controller. After all reactants were added, the suspension was stirred for 1 h to complete the reaction. Then the product was filtered, washed and dried at 60 °C in vacuum, then the dry product was calcined in a tube type electric resistance furnace at 650 °C for 15 min in argon gas flow. The scheme of preparation of kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x was described as follows (see Fig. 2 ):

In order to determine the molecular formula and structure of predecessor sample coated around kieselguhr@TiO2, the same experiment was carried out without adding kieselguhr@TiO2 in a typical preparation process: the obtained pure predecessor sample was kept at room temperature until to various measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x@CDs
ce:section.ce:para: 0.144 g of kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x, 0.0210 g of citric acid, and 0.420 mL of APS were successively dispersed in 10 mL of deionized water in a beaker. After stirring for 2 min, the mixed suspension was transferred to a 15-mL PTFE-lined stainless steel reactor and placed in an oven at 180 °C for 3 h. After the reaction was completed, the resulting mixture was centrifuged for 10 min and the supernatant was removed to obtain the kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x@CDs composite microspheres. The precipitate was repeatedly washed three times with water and ethanol, and finally dried under vacuum to obtain the product.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sample characterization
ce:section.ce:para: X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of synthesized samples were obtained using a D/Max 2550 VB/PC X-ray diffractometer with a scan speed of 1°2θ min−1 and a step size of 0.02° in the 2θ range of 5–100°. Ni-filtered CuKα radiation (λ = 1.5481A) at 40 mA and 40 kV was applied at room temperature. The Hitachi S-4800 cold-cathode field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) with an accelerating voltage of 15.0 kV was employed to examine the surface morphology of samples. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to examine the molar ratio of different valency titaniumon the surface of kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x recorded on a Kratos AXIS ULTRA DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer using an Al (Mono) X-ray as the excitation source. The amount of carbon, hydrogen, and nitrogen in the precursor sample were determined by an Elementae Vario Elemental analyzer (Modern simultaneous CHNS combustion analyzer). The content of Ti, in the precursor sample, was measured on a TJA IRIS1000 type ICP-AES analyzer. The mass percent of Ti in the coating layer of samples under different conditions were measured by EDS method.

The determination of color properties can be inferred from our published works [12,13]. Generally the CIE 1976 L* a* b* color coordinates system, which was stipulated by International Illumination Committee in 1976, is used to quantify the color characteristic of inorganic pigments. At present, this coordinate system is taken as industrial standard to appraise pigment color intensity precisely [14,15]. The color difference in two samples, and so also the chromaticity difference between two different pigments, can be shown through the ΔE*ab parameter from the L* a* b* color coordinate system. Therefore, the color instrumental measurement was used to determine L*, a* and b* value of different kinds of TiO2-x coated kieselguhr@TiO2 prepared in this work. The Max Factor Eye Shadows Black Eye shadow Single 110 Black produced by well-known beauty cosmetics company of PROCTER & GAMBLE, was taken as the standard substance and the product quality was determined by calculating ΔE*ab as follows:
Δ
E
a b
∗
=
(
L
S a m p l e
∗
−
L
Standard
∗
)
2
+
(
a
S a m p l e
∗
−
a
S
tan
d a r d
∗
)
2
+
(
b
S a m p l e
∗
−
b
S
tan
d a r d
∗
)
2
Where L* represents the luminance value, a* and b* the chromaticity coordinate: in particular a* represents red (+), and green (−), b* represents yellow (+) and blue (−).The higher ΔE*ab value, the more significant the pearly luster effect is [15]. The standard colorimetric parameters were so determined: L* = 63.83, a* = 0.86, b* = 3.99, and the colorimetric parameters of various synthesized samples determined under different conditions are listed in Tables 3–6.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Carbon, hydrogen and nitrogen (C, H and N) chemical elemental analysis
ce:section.ce:para: In order to understand the formation mechanism of the precursor, composite salt of succinic acid hydrazine titanate hydrate, the precursor was prepared by controlling the ratio of succinic acid to TiCl3 at 2:1, and at three different reaction time of 0.5, 1 and 1.5 h in the typical preparation process. The contents of C, H, and N of the obtained composite salt of succinic acid hydrazine titanate hydrate were determined by elemental analysis, and the Ti content was determined by plasma spectroscopy, the results are listed in Table 1 .

The data presented in Table 1 reveals that when the reaction time is 1.5 h, the molecular formula of the precursor is [Ti(C4H4O4)2]0.85·2Ti2O3·6N2H4·3H2O, and it can be inferred that the formation mechanism of the composite salt of succinic acid hydrazine titanate hydrate can be described as follows:
(1) 4TiCl3 + 6H2O + 6N2H4 → 2Ti2O3·6N2H4 + 12HCl
(2) 0.85 TiCl3 + 0.2125 O2 + 1.7C4H6O4 → 0.85 Ti(C4H4O4)2 + 2.55HCl + 0.425H2O

Then, the overall reaction is as follows:
4.85 TiCl3 + 8.575H2O + 6N2H4 + 1.7C4H6O4 + 0.2125 O2 → [Ti(C4H4O4)2]0.85 ·2Ti2O3·6N2H4·3H2O + 14.55 HCl

ce:section.ce:section-title: XRD characterization of the precursor
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 shows the XRD pattern of precursor [Ti(C4H4O4)2]0.85·2Ti2O3·6N2H4·3H2O. The results of indexing of the X-ray diffraction pattern for the precursor are listed in Table 2 . All of diffraction data can be indexed by a set of unit cell parameters according to the triclinic system, the calculated interplanar spacing (D) is very close to the measured value (D o), and the maximum relative percent error is less than 0.0004‰, indicating the precursor [Ti(C4H4O4)2]0.85·2Ti2O3·6N2H4·3H2O is a single phase and confirming its belonging to the triclinic system. The unit cell parameters are: a = 1.0068 nm, b = 1.1494 nm and c = 1.4294 nm as reported by our previous work [16].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of precursor molecules on HRSEM image and color parameters of the obtained samples
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 4 , the reaction time has a significant effect on the final product. When the reaction time is 1 h or less, the aggregation phenomenon is serious, and the blackness intensity of the product is low. This phenomenon can be explained as follows: when the reaction time is 1 h, the molecular formula of the formed precursor is [Ti(C4H4O4)2]0.5·2Ti2O3·3N2H4·H2O (shown in Table 2). Otherwise, if the precursor molecule is compared to the compound with molecular formula of [Ti(C4H4O4)2]0.85·2Ti2O3·6N2H4·3H2O, obtained at a reaction time of 1.5 h, the amount of [Ti(C4H4O4)2] in the precursor molecule is lower, and the amount of N2H4 is only half that of the latter. The black mixed valence titanium oxide produced by calcination and decomposition is easily oxidized in the absence of reducing gas [17]. Furthermore, the H2 generated during the thermal decomposition of N2H4 has a reducing protective effect on black titanium oxide. Accordingly, the higher content of N2H4 in the precursor leads to a higher content of black titanium oxide produced by calcination.

When the reaction time is 2 h, the dispersion of black titanium oxide produced by calcination is still relatively uniform, but the particle size of the product is reduced to about 200 nm (Fig. 4(d)). This is because as the reaction time increases, the content of N2H4 in the resulting composite salt of succinic acid hydrazine titanate hydrate also increases. As a result, the rate of thermal decomposition reaction also increases, causing also an increased development of gas generated by calcination which leads to a decrease of the particle size of black titanium oxide.

The color parameters of samples prepared at different reaction time are listed in Table 3 . The data in Table 3 reveals that when the reaction time is 0.5 h, the ΔL* value of the sample is a positive number. When the reaction time is 1.0–2.5 h, the ΔL* value of the sample is negative and the blackness of the sample reaches the highest value when the reaction time is 1.5 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermogravimetry-differential thermal analysis of precursor
ce:section.ce:para: The thermogravimetry-differential thermal analysis (TG-DTA) of a typical precursor with succinic acid as the acid source is shown in Fig. 5 . The TG-DTA results shown in Fig. 5 reveal that the thermal decomposition process of the precursor can be divided into three stages. The first endothermic peak occurs at around 100 °C and should be the result of the loss of crystal water from the precursor. The second endothermic peak occurs at around 230 °C and should correspond to the decomposition of composite salt of succinic acid hydrazine titanate hydrate. The third small exothermic peak appearing at around 480 °C is accompanied by a small weight loss. In combination with the results of the subsequent powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis, it is believed that an oxidation-reduction reaction have occurred, and titanium has changed from the intermediate valence state in the precursor to the valence state of III and IV in the product. Therefore, the temperature of 650 °C was selected as the thermal decomposition temperature of the precursor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Raman spectrum analysis of the calcined predecessor
ce:section.ce:para: The forms of elemental carbon are classified as disordered carbon and ordered carbon, which correspond to 1350 and 1580 cm−1, respectively. However, there are no peaks at these two positions in Fig. 6 , which means that the content of carbon in the sample after calcination is either too low or the sample does not contain carbon.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Powder X-ray diffraction analysis of calcined predecessor
ce:section.ce:para: The XRD spectrum of the calcined predecessor prepared by a typical preparation procedure without adding kieselguhr@TiO2 is shown in Fig. 7 . Characteristic peaks are found at, 36.93°, 37.08°, 37.80°, 48.05°, 51.89°, 54.98°, and 62.63°. These diffraction peaks are found to basically correspond to those of the standard anatase TiO2 spectrum, compared with the standard diffraction card (JCPDS file No. 21–1272 [19]. Another set of characteristic peaks are found at 25.28°, 34.92°, 39.74°, 53.86°, 62.70° and 75.07°, and are compared with standard diffraction cards (JCPDS, No. 89–4746) [19]. The result of the comparison shows that these diffraction peaks are essentially consistent with the standard spectrum of Ti2O3 [19].

The experimental data of the XRD pattern of the TiO2 phase in the calcined predecessor was indexed, and the results are listed in Table 4 . The TiO2 crystals in the calcined predecessor particles are tetragonal and have unit cell parameters a = b = 0.3790 nm, c = 0.9512 nm. The largest relative deviation between the calculated and experimental values is 0.604‰, which indicates that the TiO2 crystal in the calcined predecessor particles are a single phase with a tetragonal symmetry.

The experimental data of the XRD pattern of the Ti2O3 phase in the calcined predecessor was indexed, and the results are listed in Table 5 . The Ti2O3 crystals are hexagonal and have unit cell parameters a = b = 0.4334 nm, c = 1.3411 nm. The largest relative deviation between the calculated and experimental values is 6.05‰, which indicates that the Ti2O3 crystals in the calcined predecessor particles are a single phase with a hexagonal symmetry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the core-shell type kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x composite particles prepared by the typical preparation process
Powder X-ray diffraction analysis of kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x composite particles
ce:section.ce:para: The XRD patterns of Kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x prepared at the calcination temperature of 650 and 700 °C are shown in Fig. 8 (a) and (b), respectively. The XRD pattern of Kieselguhr@TiO2 is shown in Fig. 8(c).

Compared with the standard diffraction card (JCPDS file No. 05–0490 and No. 21–1272 [17,18]), the absorption peak at 26.7° in Fig. 8(c) is the characteristic absorption peak of SiO2. Also, the absorption peaks at 25.20°, 37.73°, 48.0°, 53.60° and 62.70° are characteristic absorption peaks of anatase TiO2, indicating that TiO2 is coated around the kieselguhr. The absorption peaks at 37.08°, 38.52°, 53.86°, 55.05°, 62.70°, 68.82°, 70.40°, and 74.92° in Fig. 8(a) are the characteristic peaks of the kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x composite particles. A comparison with the patterns shown in Fig. 7 revealed that the black titanium oxide TiO2-x has been completely coated on the surface of kieselguhr@TiO2, forming the kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x composite particles. As can be observed, no new characteristic absorption peaks are found in Fig. 8(b), indicating that the anatase TiO2 had not been converted to other crystal forms in the samples calcined at 700 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis of kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x composite particles
ce:section.ce:para: The Ti2p spectrum of the kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x sample surface is shown in Fig. 9 (a), where four peaks were obtained using Gaussian fitting routines on the raw data.

The binding energy peaks of the 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 of titanium were at 465.41 and 459.01 eV, respectively, indicating the presence of TiO2. Another pair of binding energy peaks of the 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 of titanium were at 464.63 and 459.41 eV, respectively: these peaks correspond to the Ti2O3 and 2p state of Ti3+ valence state [20].

According to the data listed in Table 6 , the ratio of the number of Ti4+ to Ti3+ ions in the titanium oxide is calculated to be 0.80, that is, the molar ratio of 2TiO2 to Ti2O3 is 0.80, so the composition of the TiO2-x sample can be assumed to be: 2TiO2·1.25Ti2O3.

The Si2p XPS integral chart of kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x sample surface is shown in Fig. 9(b). The binding energy peak of the 2p of Si was 102.70 eV, demonstrating that the occurrence of silica resulted from the kieselguhr.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) measurements of Kieselguhr@TiO2 and Kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x
ce:section.ce:para: The mass percent of Ti in the coating layer of kieselguhr@TiO2 and kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x in a typical preparation process were measured by EDS analysis, and the EDS patterns of Ti coating are shown as Fig. 10 (a) and Fig. 10(b). The patterns in Fig. 10(a) show that the coating layer of kieselguhr@TiO2 contains O, Ti, and Si, and molar ratio of Ti/Si is 1.170, indicating that TiO2 is directly coated on the surface of the kieselguhr. However, the molar ratio of Ti/Si for the coating layer of kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x increased to 2.494 after coating of TiO2-x, namely TiO2-x had been coated on the kieselguhr@TiO2. Additionally, the atomic number ratio of oxygen to titanium in TiO2-x on the surface of kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x is about (50.94–8.07 × 2)/20.13 = 1.73 by considering the Si binding two oxygen, which is nearly in accordance with the atomic ratio of oxygen and titanium in the composition of 2TiO2·Ti2O3. This confirms that the chemical formula of the black titanium oxide is probably 2TiO2·Ti2O3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: High-resolution scanning electron microscopy characterization of Kieselguhr, Kieselguhr@TiO2 and Kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x
ce:section.ce:para: The image displayed in Fig. 11 (a) shows that the majority of the diatom bodies in kieselguhr was disc-shaped with capillaries of different sizes inside the disc, but a small amount of the diatom bodies in kieselguhr was single or double joint cylindrical with capillaries of different size on the cylinder wall. In addition, there were a large amount of fragments of diatom bodies distributed around disc-shaped and cylindrical diatom body. The image presented in Fig. 11(b) reveals that numerous pores of about 350 nm were uniformly distributed on the surface of the disc-shaped diatom bodies.

High-resolution scanning electron microscopy (HRSEM) images of kieselguhr@TiO2 are shown in Fig. 11(c) and (d). The image in Fig. 11(c) reveals that particles of about 75 nm TiO2 were evenly coated on the surface of the kieselguhr. The image in Fig. 11(d) indicates that the surface of the kieselguhr was coated by TiO2 of about 105 nm. This shows that the TiO2 coated on the surface of the kieselguhr prepared by the sol-gel deposition method has a nano-scale size, but the operating conditions should be strictly controlled.

The HRSEM images of kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x are shown in Fig. 11(e) and (f). The surface of kieselguhr@TiO2 was uniformly coated with mixed valence titanium oxide TiO2-x of about 320–400 nm. Its surface is smooth and dense, indicating that the surface of kieselguhr@TiO2 is coated with submicron titanium oxides TiO2-x. This is because the co-precipitation plus hydrazine salt pyrolysis route is different from the sol-gel route. The hydrazine salt pyrolysis route is liable to produce reducing gas during the formation of the particles, leading to the generation of sub-micron sized mixed valence TiO2-x.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of the conditions parameters on the morphology and color intensity
Effects of the acid/TiCl3 ratio on the morphology and color intensity
ce:section.ce:para: The X-ray diffraction patterns of kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x prepared at different acid/TiCl3 ratios are shown in Fig. 12 . The patterns reveal that the characteristic peak of amorphous silica at 21.4°, corresponding to kieselguhr, was present in all XRD patterns. When the acid/TiCl3 ratio was less than 2, there were no Ti2O3 diffraction peaks in the XRD pattern of the product. Additionally, the corresponding characteristic diffraction peaks of anatase TiO2 were found only at 2θ = 25.28°, 36.93°, 37.80°, 47.97°, 51.89°, 54.98°, and 62.63°. When the acid/TiCl3 ratio was equal to 2 or larger, the Ti2O3 characteristic diffraction peaks appeared at 2θ = 18.32°, 34.92°, 37.08°, 39.74°, 51.88°, 62.70°, and 75.07° in the XRD pattern of the product, and the characteristic diffraction peaks of anatase TiO2 appeared at 2θ = 25.28°, 36.93°, 37.80°, 47.97°, 51.89°54.98° and 62.63°.

The HRSEM patterns of TiO2-x with different acid/TiCl3 ratio are shown in Fig. 13 . The acid/TiCl3 ratio played a major role on the particle size, morphology and color intensity of the final product. When the acid/TiCl3 ratio was greater than or less than 2:1, the product had a smaller particle size of 140–180 nm. However, when the acid/TiCl3 ratio was 2:1, the product had a larger particle size, of about 180–240 nm, and the image displayed in Fig. 13(c) shows that the particles were smooth and uniform.

The colorimetric parameters of the samples with different acid/TiCl3 ratio are listed in Table 7 . These parameters indicate that when the acid/TiCl3 ratio is less than 2:1, the ΔL* value of the sample is positive leading to a product exhibiting gray color. However, when the acid/TiCl3 ratio is greater than or equal to 2:1, the ΔL* value of the sample is negative. This may be due to the presence of trivalent titanium ions in the form of complexes in the organic acid solution. When the organic acid concentration is low, some of the trivalent titanium reacts with sodium hydroxide to form precipitates. Eventually, titanium dioxide is produced after calcination and the color of the product turns from black to gray.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of pH on the morphology and color intensity
ce:section.ce:para: When the pH value is low, the coating rate is slow, and the coating layer was not compact and uniform, this is because the OH− in the solution did not neutralize Ti3+, thus, more hydrated Ti3+ easily produced complex multi-core polymer, and ultimately deposited on the surface of the kieselguhr@TiO2 in the form of stacking faults.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of the amount of hydrazine hydrate on the morphology and color intensity
Effects of the concentration of sodium hydroxide on the morphology and color intensity
ce:section.ce:para: The XRD patterns of the kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x prepared with different amounts of hydrazine hydrate are shown in Fig. 15 . When the amount of hydrazine hydrate (85.0% w/w) was less than 10 mL, the corresponding characteristic diffraction peaks of the anatase TiO2 appeared at 2θ = 25.28°, 36.93°, 37.80°, 47.96°, 51.90°, and 62.63° in the XRD pattern of the obtained samples indicating that the coating of the product was anatase TiO2: the product exhibited a gray or gray white color, as shown in Table 9 . The ΔL* of the obtained product has a positive value, indicating that it had lower blackness intensity than that of the standard sample. The reason for this feature is that hydrazine hydrate plays a role in the reduction of titanium (IV), thus the insufficient amount of hydrazine hydrate was not able to reduce titanium (IV) to low-valent titanium.

When the amount of hydrazine hydrate (85.0% w/w) was more than 10 mL, the characteristic diffraction peaks of Ti2O3 appeared at 2θ = 34.92°, 37.08°, 39.94°, and 62.70° in the XRD pattern of the obtained product and the product appeared black, as shown in Table 9. The ΔL* of the obtained product had, now, a negative value, indicating that it had higher blackness intensity than that of the standard sample: it is likely that the Ti2O3 generated from the reduction of titanium (IV) by hydrazine hydrate might result in the color of product becoming darker.

The amount of hydrazine hydrate also has a great influence on the morphology of the final product. As shown in Fig. 16 (a) and (b), when the amount of hydrazine hydrate (85.0% w/w) was less than 10 mL, relatively large aggregated particles in the range of 0.5–1.5 μm, as well as separated particles, are well visible. However, as showed in the images displayed in Fig. 16(c), (d) and (e), when the amount of hydrazine hydrate was equal to 10 mL or more, homogeneously dispersed spherical, uniform particles of size in the range of 80–120 nm appeared: these correspond to black TiO2-x nanoparticles, which result in a higher degree of blackness intensity of the final product.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of the reaction temperature on the morphology and color intensity
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 17 , the temperature had a significant effect on the morphology of the final product. When the reaction temperature was less than 35 °C or higher than 55 °C, most of the agglomerated particles in the range of 400–600 nm are observed, as reported in Fig. 17(a); while separated nonhomogeneous particles in the range of 600–1100 nm are present in the images displayed in Fig. 17(e). When the reaction temperature was within the range of 35–45 °C, the coating surface was compact, smooth and dense, the ΔL* of the obtained product reached the most negative value, as shown in Table 10 , indicating that it had higher blackness intensity than that of the standard sample. Thus, the temperature of 35–45 °C was beneficial to the generation of black TiO2-x nanoparticles. The lower reaction temperature can make the TiO2-x deposition rate very slow, thus the deposition of particles is too small to make the surface energy larger and the particles tend to reunite: This causes the ΔL* of the obtained product to become a small negative value. However, the higher reaction temperature might accelerate the precipitation of Ti3+, leading to the deposition rate to become too fast, which in turn causes agglomeration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of the calcination temperature on the morphology and color intensity
ce:section.ce:para: The HRSEM images of kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x obtained at different calcination temperatures are shown in Fig. 18 . Specifically, as well displayed in Fig. 18(a), when the calcination temperature was 600 °C, some aggregated particles, with the size in the range of 650–800 nm, and separated particles, with the size in the range of 150–200 nm, were non-uniformly dispersed. A 50 °C increase in the rising calcination temperature made the coating layer look smooth and dense (Fig. 18(b)), and the well dispersed nanoparticles had a size of about 170–220 nm. The likely reason for this is that the thermal decomposition of the precursor began at 650 °C, and the temperature below 650 °C was not beneficial to thermal decomposition of the precursor into the coating layer of black TiO2-x, which is in good agreement with the TG-DTA results. The results of the color measurement also confirmed these findings (Table 10), the ΔL* value of the product calcined at 600 °C was positive. On the contrary, the ΔL* value of the product calcined at 650 °C was more negative, having lower brightness and higher blackness intensity, which corresponds to black TiO2-x nanoparticles.

When the calcination temperature rose to 700 °C, the well-dispersed particles with the size in the range of 250–300 nm and a small amount of flake shaped kieselguhr fragments are present in the image displayed in Fig. 18(c). Also, as shown in Table 11 , the ΔL* of the product showed a smaller negative value, which probably corresponded to small amount of black TiO2-x nanoparticles.

Higher temperatures might accelerate thermal decomposition of the precursor. Additionally, certain amount of hydrazine hydrate in the precursor only produces limited reducing gas around the product. Thus, when the reducing gas is quickly volatilized, due to the higher temperature, some product would not receive the protection from the reducing gas, as results in the oxidation of some generated black TiO2-x nanoparticles into TiO2 again.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon dots coated composite pigment (kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x@CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths are shown in Fig. 19 (a), where an intense broad band is present in the interval range of 340–400 nm with a maximum peak at 260 nm and almost without any displacement at the emission spectrum peak. Upon excitation with a 260 nm light, the CDs, kieselguhr@CDs and kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x@CDs each reached their maximum emission intensity. As shown in Fig. 19(b), the fluorescence emission intensity of kieselguhr@CDs was decreased by a factor of two, compared to bare CDs. However, the fluorescence emission intensity of kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x@CDs was far lower than that of kieselguhr@CDs, probably due to fluorescence quenching by the shell of TiO2-x nanoparticles around kieselguhr@TiO2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity against mouse peritoneal macrophages of kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x@CDs composite particles
ce:section.ce:para: The in vitro cytotoxicity against mouse peritoneal macrophages was investigated to evaluate the potential application for kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x@CDs composite particles as a green and environmentally friendly pearlescent pigment (Fig. 20 ). The kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x@CDs composite particles were selected as the model, and studied under conditions of 24 h and 48 h incubation of mouse peritoneal macrophages with different concentrations of sample with 0, 25, 50, 100, 150, and 200 μg mL−1. The results indicate that kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x@CDs composite particles show low cytotoxicity to the mouse peritoneal macrophages with different concentrations. Even though the concentration of sample reached up to 200 μg mL−1, the surviving fraction of mouse peritoneal macrophages cells is still up to 73% for 24 h and 71% for 48 h, indicating that kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x@CDs composite particles show obvious low cytotoxicity against mouse peritoneal macrophages. No explanation can be given for the cytotoxic effects of the kieselguhr products, but it is possible that the surface hydroxyl groups or surface charge could play a part in membrane disruption [21].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: The “green” core-shell type kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x composite particles pearlescent pigment were prepared via sol-gel deposition of TiO2 coated on the surface of kieselguhr, and then co-precipitation plus hydrazine salt pyrolysis route was employed to coat TiO2-x on the kieselguhr@TiO2. It can be inferred that the precursor of hydrazine salt pyrolysis route [Ti(C4H4O4)2]0.85·2Ti2O3·6N2H4·3H2O was obtained at the optimum reaction time of 1.5 h and 40 °C and the coating layer became smooth and dense, due to the fact that deposition rate of smooth coating depended on reaction temperature. If the reaction time was not more than 1 h, less amount of [Ti(C4H4O4)2] and N2H4 in precursor molecule would make coating layer unsmooth and considerably lower color intensity, with pale black or gray. TG-DTA analysis shows that the precursor coated on the kieselguhr@TiO2 should be calcined at 650 °C for 15 min under argon atmosphere. Higher or lower temperature would be not beneficial to formation of smooth and dense coating layer, due to limited amount of hydrazine hydrate in the precursor. According to molecular formula of the precursor, the optimum succinic acid/TiCl3 and hydrazine hydrate amount were kept at 2:1 and 10 mL respectively. Moreover, the concentration of dropped NaOH and the pH of the solution should be controlled 0.5–1.0 mol/L and 4.0–4.5 respectively. In addition, the obtained black kieselguhr@TiO2@TiO2-x@CDs composite particles show obvious low cytotoxicity against mouse peritoneal macrophages, demonstrating that this pigment can be considered as a green and environmentally friendly pearlescent pigment.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 29201004) and to Mr Xiang-nong Liu, at the Analysis & Testing Center, Yangzhou University for help in sample characterization.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Structural change of titanium oxide evaporated films during in situ heating in an electron microscope

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Yamada, I. Shiota, K. Yoshida, Structural change of titanium oxide evaporated films during in situ heating in an electron microscope, J. Vac. Sci. Technol. 11(5) (1993) 2642-2648.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In vitro cytotoxicity and quantitative silica analysis of diatomaceous earth products

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Bye, R. Davies, D. M. Griffiths, B. Gylseth, C. B. Moncrieff, In vitro cytotoxicity and quantitative silica analysis of diatomaceous earth products, British J. Ind. Med. 41(2) (1984) 22.
